attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of progressive red-brown rash on his legs and feet. He has also noticed similar lesions in his mouth. He reports unintentional weight loss of 5 kg over the past two months and night sweats. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He works as a graphic designer and has no relevant travel history. He recently broke up with his girlfriend after finding out that she had been having unprotected sexual intercourse with multiple casual partners during their relationship.

On examination, he appears thin but well. His vital signs are within normal limits. There are multiple non-tender, non-blanching, red-brown papules and plaques on his lower legs and feet. Similar lesions are visible on his hard palate. There is generalised lymphadenopathy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with characteristic features of Kaposi sarcoma, including purple-brown patches affecting both skin and oral mucosa, along with constitutional symptoms suggesting underlying immunocompromise. The presentation strongly suggests AIDS-associated Kaposi sarcoma, and HIV testing would be indicated. Kaposi sarcoma is caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8) infection is the most common malignancy in people living with HIV. In East and Central Africa, there is up to 90% rate of infection of HHV-8 in adults with HIV. It typically presents with non-blanching vascular nodules, plaques, papules or patches that can affect anywhere on the body, including the genitals and mouth, with accompanying lymphoedema.

Sweet syndrome typically presents with tender, erythematous plaques often associated with fever and neutrophilia. The lesions are usually painful and more pink or red rather than purple-brown. It does not typically affect mucous membranes.

Pyogenic granuloma presents as a solitary, rapidly growing, friable vascular nodule that bleeds easily. It does not present with multiple lesions or constitutional symptoms, and oral involvement would be unusual.

Bacillary angiomatosis, caused by Bartonella species, can present similarly to Kaposi sarcoma in HIV-positive patients. However, the lesions are typically more red and dome-shaped, and are often tender. They also tend to be more rapidly growing than Kaposi sarcoma.

Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma typically presents with scaly patches and plaques that are more red or pink in colour. While it can be associated with constitutional symptoms, the characteristic purple-brown colour and oral involvement would be unusual.
learningPoints: Kaposi sarcoma commonly presents as purple-brown macules or nodules in immunocompromised patients.
HIV status should be considered in patients presenting with unexplained vascular skin lesions.
references: Altman K, Vanness E, Westergaard RP. Cutaneous manifestations of human immunodeficiency virus: a clinical update. Curr Infect Dis Rep. 2015.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4447481/

Guidelines on the treatment of skin and oral HIV-associated conditions in children and adults.  World Health Organization. 2014.  Available at: https://iris.who.int/bitstream/handle/10665/136863/9789241548915_eng.pdf?sequence=1
